interactions.summary: Jiang continues the opening model by tying mineral access and global war to Chinese dollar dependence.
The host challenges whether coercion destroys the trust required for a durable dollar order and asks how to read the strategic contradiction.
Jiang answers that the hegemon insists on Chinese subordination, but mutual dependence means both sides can wreck one another.
The host reframes the conflict as ideological hegemony and notes that Venezuela is about denying rivals access, not merely securing American supply.

claims.claim: Jiang says Trump’s wider war pattern aims to strangle China’s access not only to oil but also to strategic minerals needed for EVs and AI.
Jiang agrees that coercing China while seeking trust is contradictory, but says the contradiction persists because the United States insists on hegemony rather than equality.
Jiang says China cannot simply replace Middle Eastern and Western Hemisphere oil with Russian supply because Russia lacks the capacity and infrastructure.
Jiang says China can still destabilize the dollar, citing early-2026 silver export restrictions and resulting stress in US financial markets.
Jiang models the US-China rivalry as mutually assured destruction in which pushing the ladder too far in either direction makes both sides fall.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang turns US-China interdependence into a ladder over an abyss.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Imagine a ladder over an abyss
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is his clearest image for rivalry that cannot be won by humiliating the other side.

signature_moments.moment: War becomes a resource choke strategy rather than a sequence of isolated conflicts.
signature_moments.source_phrase: conducting all these wars around the world in order to strangle the Chinese economy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the heat of Jiang’s causal claim instead of reducing it to trade competition.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang uses the nuclear-age phrase for US-China financial and energy interdependence.
The host uses this for a US posture oscillating between trust-building and coercion.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang treats China’s silver export restriction as an early-2026 event already signaling available Chinese financial retaliation tools.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR is noisy around some commodity-market wording, but the substantive point is that China used a silver restriction to create US market stress.
